transcript.transcript: I didn't think that was too much. They didn't think it was kind of nonsense to... I don't know what they said, though. I know. I remember when this happened. Do you remember when the radio first came in? Well, I don't know. We didn't have a radio anyways. Do you remember hearing it in town or something? Yes. What did you think of that? I thought it was wonderful. Pretty extraordinary, I'd imagine that. Yeah. Pretty amazing. So, you got married And you had how many children? Three daughters. Three daughters. Now, how did most of them like growing up with no electricity and having to get wood for heat? Well, you know, when the person doesn't know nothing else, they're satisfied. We got our electricity in 1938. Oh. Well, I bet that was wonderful. Oh, sure it was. I bet that was pretty exciting. Then you got electric lights and... Yeah. Well, we had a battery radio before. Oh, battery radio? Yeah, but then we got another one. But then we got a refrigerator. So what did you keep your stuff cold in? Well, I don't know. What, in a root cellar? Well, we tried. Well, you can't really keep them in a root cellar like some foods. Like milk. We Just try to have water and just stand in the water. Cold water. Oh, OK. We'll have to try that. Yes, you will! I don't know what we're going to do. OK. Now, that brings us up to about the war, the Second World War. You probably remember Pearl Harbor and that. Yes, I remember that. Now, were there any Japanese Americans in this area? No, not at all of them. Not that I know of, anyway. It seems like there's quite a few Japanese here now. Yeah, but we didn't know about them so much so we stayed here. Oh, yeah, I guess so. So, did your... How did the... What were the war years like? I mean, how did they affect you and your family? Well, they affected us quite bad because our house burned down. We couldn't hardly get nothing to just rebuild the house. Your house burned down? Yes. And we couldn't hardly even buy stuff to rebuild the house and get Clothing and everything like that. Couldn't even get any wood? Well, wood, we cut wood up on our own land. Oh. Had plenty of that. Like shoes and clothing, that was real hard to get. And building materials, we had some old, cheesy kind of stuff, North Fork River. The government was selling them, So we finally got one of those that we tore down and hauled home. And we had to drive about 50 miles to get them. What was this now? The old city camp. Oh, the civilian conservation camp. Oh, that's right. They used to have them up there. So how did your house burn down? Well, we had a safe roof on our house and I guess just some people went through some of the chimney and caught fire just early in the spring. Was there anybody in the house? Not sure. One of my daughters and Tommy and Lon, their baby and I was in the house when it cut fire. And you all got out, okay. Oh, yes we got out. Part of our stuff out. Most of it burned. Oh, that's terrible. No insurance, huh? Very little. Very little. There's about four of us that come to cut the rebuild. Boy, that's setback, huh? Oh, it's a big one. Setback. So what did you live while you were getting your other house built? I only put up a tent. Put up a tent? Yes, I only lived for a while. And then by fall, it was on May the 18th, 1913. By fall and my, we got the shell of a house. This is all a lot of the stuff that you've had on for a long time, burning up on the wall? Oh, yes, and the worst thing of all is there's been so many pictures that I've never replaced, please. Right. That's really a shame. That was probably the really low point, huh? Oh, that's just the... It's all up hill? I mean, it was all up from there, I can imagine. There's what? That's what I can imagine. Oh, now what kind of, Didn't you tell Donnie you had a, gas refrigerator? No. Was it a gas washing machine? Oh yes, we had a gas washing machine. And we got electricity, we bought an electric motor. And I was telling her that when we sold it, gas motors, that some people, to their summer homes, they pumped their water with it. A gas washing machine? Oh, yeah. Just gasoline. That's really amazing. Well, you know, it's just about like a lawnmower motor. I bet that was really nice once you got a washing machine, huh? Oh, sure. I mean, a lot of... It really saves you a lot of work. Oh, yeah. I used to wash clothes before, just on the washboard. Just on the washboard, huh? Uh-huh. Boy, must have been hard work being a mother. Well, it was just about a day's job washing clothes. I imagine. Well, did you have any of your children at home? What do you mean at home? I mean, did you have birth to them at home? Oh, yes. All of them. All of them? Yeah. You had birth to them at home. Well, did your husband help you? There was a midwife there. Oh, was there? The first one we had a doctor. Then it was midwife. Oh. So did your husband help you much? How could he? Well, I don't know. He could actually help the midwife. Ha ha. I mean, was he kind of freaked out by the whole thing, or did he have it under control? I don't know. He had it... ha ha. Oh, he had it under control. That's amazing, all four children at home. Not four, three. Oh, three. All right. Mrs. Wright? Five. Five? Really? I can't keep my facts straight. Okay. Now, when did you move to Coeur d'Alene? 1956. 1956. Now what was it like when you first moved here? Well, just about the same as it is now. We've just grown up more, failed us more. I mean, how many people do you think were here then? Well, there was supposed to be around 16, 000. There? Oh, I didn't realize it was that big. How many is it now? Oh, 18, 000. Oh. Well, that's really heavy. Of course, it's built outside the city limits so much that it's not counted. Oh, I see. All surrounding areas. Mrs. Wright, did you move here at the same time? When did you move to Coeur d'Alene? What? When did you move to Coeur d'Alene? When we moved? What... 1955. 1955? Just retired here? No? You know you moved? And now we've moved, but still the Coeur d'Alenes. Yeah. Different location now. So you've been pretty satisfied with your life? You've been, you've led a really full life then, huh? Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. Raised on the farm. Yeah. And married and have three children. Yeah, it seemed a lot of hard work. Seemed a lot of hard work, boy, I bet you. We have it quite a bit easier today, haven't we? Well, you don't know nothing about work if you didn't like it. That's really true, though. I mean, that's really true. Just from what you've been telling us. Oh, say, I've got a story to tell you. And I used to help my husband mowing the hay over there in our place. He had a mowing machine, and this one time was there was a skunk in the hay. Cut all of its legs with a mowing machine. And as I wasn't alone in my front door, now there was a couple more weeks to go. I went to the house and got a 22-hundred shot of it, and packed that away and finished my job. That's pretty good. Oh, you can imagine the odors that there was around him. So I just took my clothes off and put them in the wood shed. So he sprayed you right when you cut his legs off, huh? Well, naturally they gave him a spray. Even if you don't, if you see stirrup on there, they get scared of us. They spray. Well, have you ever been in concerts around here, you know what that smells like. The whole Canyon smell. Oh, that's pretty good. So you had to, you had to, You went to the house and got your gun and shot it, and then you went and took your clothes off in the woodshed? Did you have to burn those clothes? No. And when you leave them outside, the air takes the odor off. How long does it take? Oh, I don't know, a week? A week? Maybe a week. So how long does it take to get the odor off of you? Well, I didn't know that it was going to be on me. But didn't the rest of your family notice? Oh, sure. They said, Mom, I wonder what you've been doing. They could tell by this odor. I guess that's really bad, Well... I'm Brian Markey, and today is March 20, 1976. And I was talking with Mrs. Hilda Wright and Mrs. Mamie Johnson.

lines.line: > The conversation begins with reminiscing about past experiences, such as the introduction of the radio. The participants express their wonder and amazement at the emerging technology and discuss the lack of certain amenities like electricity during their early life.

metadata.title: Introduction and Early Memories
metadata.synopsis: The conversation begins with reminiscing about past experiences, such as the introduction of the radio. The participants express their wonder and amazement at the emerging technology and discuss the lack of certain amenities like electricity during their early life.

lines.line: > The discussion shifts to family life, highlighting how the lack of electricity and reliance on wood for heat was normal for them. They recall the transition to electricity and the impact it had, including the use of battery-powered radios and early refrigerators.
** family life, electricity, battery radio, refrigerator **
keywords: ** family life, electricity, battery radio, refrigerator **

metadata.title: Family Life Without Modern Conveniences
metadata.synopsis: The discussion shifts to family life, highlighting how the lack of electricity and reliance on wood for heat was normal for them. They recall the transition to electricity and the impact it had, including the use of battery-powered radios and early refrigerators.
metadata.keywords: family life, electricity, battery radio, refrigerator

lines.line: > The participants reflect on how World War II affected them personally, including a house fire due to a chimney mishap. They discuss the difficulties in rebuilding, such as acquiring materials and basic necessities during wartime.
** World War II, house fire, rebuilding, wartime shortages **
keywords: ** World War II, house fire, rebuilding, wartime shortages **

metadata.title: Impact of World War II
metadata.synopsis: The participants reflect on how World War II affected them personally, including a house fire due to a chimney mishap. They discuss the difficulties in rebuilding, such as acquiring materials and basic necessities during wartime.
metadata.keywords: World War II, house fire, rebuilding, wartime shortages

lines.line: > They share their experiences of living conditions after the fire, including residing in a tent and gradually rebuilding their home. The conversation touches on home births of their children, with the presence of a midwife.
** living conditions, home births, midwife, tent living **
keywords: ** living conditions, home births, midwife, tent living **

metadata.title: Living Arrangements and Home Births
metadata.synopsis: They share their experiences of living conditions after the fire, including residing in a tent and gradually rebuilding their home. The conversation touches on home births of their children, with the presence of a midwife.
metadata.keywords: living conditions, home births, midwife, tent living

lines.line: > The narrative transitions to their move to Coeur d'Alene in 1956, discussing the changes and growth of the town from when they first arrived. They reflect on their satisfaction with their lives, having led a full life on the farm and raising a family.
** Coeur d'Alene, relocation, town growth, farm life **

** farm life, skunk, hard work, humor **

story.title: Johnson_Mayme_OH 1653_B
story.description: Sisters Mayme Johnson (1901-1996) and Hilda Wright (1890-1982) are interviewed by Brian Markey on their lives. Interviewed March 3, 1976.
